Name: cuda_kernel_optimization
Title: Hardware-Aware CUDA Kernel Optimization for Efficient Inference on Commodity GPUs
Short Hypothesis: By optimizing CUDA kernels for efficient inference on commodity GPUs, we can achieve significant improvements in wall-clock latency and tokens-per-second, leading to better performance and efficiency in real-world applications.
Related Work: Previous work has focused on spectral latent steering, geometric interference cancellation, and spectral quantization routing. However, these methods often rely on theoretical bounds and do not provide a clear, hardware-faithful evaluation of their proposals. In contrast, our approach focuses on empirical tuning and measurement of kernel performance, using tools like nvprof and Nsight to optimize kernel design and memory access patterns.
Abstract: This proposal presents a hardware-aware approach to optimizing CUDA kernels for efficient inference on commodity GPUs. By using empirical tuning and measurement of kernel performance, we aim to achieve significant improvements in wall-clock latency and tokens-per-second. Our approach involves optimizing kernel design, memory access patterns, and register allocation to minimize memory bandwidth utilization and maximize arithmetic intensity. We will evaluate our optimized kernels using nvprof and Nsight, and report results on a range of benchmarks, including latency, throughput, and energy per token.
Experiments: Optimize kernel design for coalesced memory access and minimal bank conflicts
Tune register allocation to minimize register spill and maximize occupancy
Evaluate the impact of different memory access patterns on kernel performance
Compare the performance of our optimized kernels to state-of-the-art baselines
Measure the energy efficiency of our optimized kernels using NVIDIA's energy measurement tools
Risk Factors and Limitations: The optimization process may require significant expertise in CUDA programming and kernel optimization
The results may be specific to the particular GPU architecture and may not generalize to other platforms
The optimization process may not always lead to significant improvements in performance, and may require careful tuning and experimentation
